coredata.dc:title: Dielectric properties of graphene quantum dot-cobalt ferrite-poly(vinylidene fluoride) ternary composites
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Tian
Liu, Bo
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot-cobalt ferrite-poly(vinylidene fluoride) (GQD-CFO-PVDF) ternary composites were prepared with GQD and CFO as co-fillers. One filler is conducting; the otherfiller is nonconducting and magnetic. The morphology of nanofillers was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) patterns of CFO-PVDF and CFO-GQD-PVDF composites implied the α-phase to β-phase crystalline transformation of PVDF. The dielectric constant of the CFO-GQD-PVDF ternary composite was much higher than that of the CFO-PVDF composites and the dielectric loss of CFO-GQD-PVDF was much lower than that of the GQD-PVDF composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly(vinylidene fluoride)
Graphene quantum dot
CoFe2O4
Polymeric composites
Electrical properties

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51603102).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of CFO, (b) XRD patterns of PVDF, 20%CFO-PVDF and 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF, and (c) FT-IR patterns of PVDF, 20%CFO-PVDF and 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF.
(a) TEM of CFO, (b) SEAD picture of CFO, and (c) TEM image of GQD, and (d) AFM topography of GQD deposited on p-Si substrate.

(a) Dependence of dielectric constant and (b) dielectric loss of PVDF and CFO-PVDF composites on frequency. (c) Dependence of dielectric constant and (d) dielectric loss of 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF composite on frequency. (e) Dependence of dielectric constant and (f) dielectric loss of GQD-PVDF composites on frequency.

head.ce:title: Dielectric properties of graphene quantum dot-cobalt ferrite-poly(vinylidene fluoride) ternary composites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Technology, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing 210016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Technology
Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dot-cobalt ferrite-poly(vinylidene fluoride) (GQD-CFO-PVDF) ternary composites were prepared with GQD and CFO as co-fillers. One filler is conducting; the otherfiller is nonconducting and magnetic. The morphology of nanofillers was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) patterns of CFO-PVDF and CFO-GQD-PVDF composites implied the α-phase to β-phase crystalline transformation of PVDF. The dielectric constant of the CFO-GQD-PVDF ternary composite was much higher than that of the CFO-PVDF composites and the dielectric loss of CFO-GQD-PVDF was much lower than that of the GQD-PVDF composites.

ce:section.ce:para: As the electronics industry in the future will evolve to the direction of miniaturization, the embedded capacitors have drawn extensive attention of researchers and engineers. Embedded capacitors will replace current discrete capacitors and can be extensively used in next generation modern electronics [1–3]. Both high dielectric constant and low dielectric loss in dielectrics are highly desired in embedded capacitor applications [4]. Polymer based dielectric composite is one kind of ideal embedded capacitor dielectric material [5–8].

PVDF has been widely used as electroactive polymer materials, such as sensors, actuators, energy harvesting, and embedded capacitors field, due to its excellent dielectric, piezoelectric, pyroelectric, and ferroelectric properties [9]. Based on the filler catalogues, PVDF based dielectric composites can be divided into two main kinds, PVDF-nonconducting filler composites and PVDF-conducting filler composites [10]. Blending PVDF with high dielectric constant nonconducting ceramic nanoparticles, such as BaTiO3 [11] and Pb(Zr,Ti)O3 [12], can increase the dielectric constant of PVDF composite. However, high concentration of the ceramic nanoparticles (often over 50%) is necessary to arriving large dielectric constant in the ceramic-PVDF composites, which causes severe damage to the processability, flexibility, and electrical performance of polymer composites [13]. Blending PVDF with conducting nanoparticles, such as nanometals [14] and nanocarbons [15], can greatly increase the dielectric constant with very small amount. However, it is also quite easy to form a conducting network in composite which leads to an extremely high dielectric loss. To counter these disadvantages, ternary composites consisting of conducting filler, nonconducting filler, and polymer matrix have come under consideration as they can ensure a high dielectric constant and low dielectric loss without breaking down the mechanical properties of the composites [16].

In this study, we prepared a graphene quantum dot-cobalt ferrite-poly(vinylidene fluoride) ternary composite with high dielectric constant and low dielectric loss. Cobalt ferrite was chosen as the nonconducting filler because of its magnetic and dielectric properties. Graphene quantum dot was chosen as conducting filler because of its excellent electrical performance and very small size. Co-filling of graphene quantum dot and cobalt ferrite nanoparticle creates a three-dimensional structure that guarantees a composite material with both good flexibility and high dielectric constant, and it can also ensure a reduced dielectric loss.

PVDF was supplied by Shanghai 3F new Materials Tech Co., Ltd and graphene quantum dot was supplied by Nanjing XF NANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. CoFe2O4 nanoparticles were synthesized by a simple co-precipitation method, by dissolving CoCl2·4H2O and FeCl3·6H2O in deionized water in the required mole proportion (1:2) with vigorous stirring for 2h at 60°C. Graphene quantum dot-cobalt ferrite-poly(vinylidene fluoride) ternary composites were prepared using solution cast method as described in our previous paper [17].

XRD patterns were recorded by a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. FT-IR spectra were measured on Nicolet NEXUS 470 fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. TEM images were taken by a FEI Tecnai G2 transmission electron microscope. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were taken by a NT-MDT atomic force microscope. The dielectric properties were obtained by a Novocontrol broadband dielectric/impedance spectrometer.

Fig. 1 a showed XRD analysis, in which all the diffraction peaks could be indexed to the spinel CoFe2O4 (JCPDS No. 22-1086), indicating the formation of the single-phase. As shown in the XRD pattern, the diffraction peaks could be assigned to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of CFO [18]. The TEM image of our synthesized CFO (Fig. 2 a) showed the adopted co-precipitation method yielded into nanoparticles with 20–50nm size and with some extent of agglomerations. Fig. 2b showed the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CFO. The pattern presented several complete diffraction rings, indicating that there were abundant nanocrystalline particles in the selected area. However, the weak rings indicated that the degree of crystallization of CFO crystal was poor. This was in good agreement with XRD analysis. Fig. 2c was the TEM picture and Fig. 2d was the AFM image of GQD. From the TEM and AFM images we could get the information that the GQD sample used in this experiment was composed of lots of tiny graphene nanosheets. Theparticle size was, on the whole, small and uniform. The height and size of the chosen GQD nanosheet in AFM image was ca. 1.8 and 10nm, respectively, corresponding to 3–4 layers.

Fig. 1b showed XRD patterns of PVDF, 20%CFO-PVDF and 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF. The fine crystalline structure and the variation could be derived from the XRD spectra. For neat PVDF, three α-phase related peaks at 2θ=18.6° (020), 20.0° (210) and 26.6° (110) are well revealed. Meanwhile, the peak at 2θ=20.6° emerging for samples 20%CFO-PVDF and 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF, corresponds to (110) and (200) reflection of the β-phase [19]. Moreover, the peak at 2θ=26.6° disappeared for these two samples, implying that the α- to β-phase crystalline transformation occurred for 20%CFO-PVDF and 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF. All the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of CFO were obviously shown in XRD spectra of 20%CFO-PVDF and 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF. Fig. 1c showed FT-IR spectra of PVDF, 20%CFO-PVDF and 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF. The IR absorption bands at 614, 763 and 975cm−1 were characteristic bands for the α-phase, and the β-phase related bands were at 840, and 1179cm−1. It was noteworthy that the α-phase related peaks at 614, 763 and 975cm−1 was clearly seen in PVDF, which decreased greatly in 20%CFO-PVDF and 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF. The significant increase of β-phase in both 20%CFO-PVDF and 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF suggested that the positive -CH2 charge density of the PVDF chains had strong interactions with the negatively charged surface of CFO, which could lead the polymer chains to align on the surface of the nanoparticle in the extended TTTT conformation and therefore promoting the crystal formation of β-phase [20].

Fig. 3 compared the three kinds of dielectric-frequency curves. The frequency dependence of the dielectric constant and dielectric loss of the CFO-PVDF composites was presented in Fig. 3a and b, respectively. A relative slight decrease in the dielectric constant of the composites was observed with the increase in frequency from 0.1Hz to 10MHz. The dielectric constant of the CFO-PVDF composites was below 100 under all the showed frequency. However, dielectric loss decreased initially and then increased after 10kHz (Fig. 3b), which could be attributed to the space charge effect [21]. The frequency dependence of the dielectric constant and dielectric loss of the GQD-PVDF composites was presented in Fig. 3e and f, respectively. A sharp decrease in the dielectric constant of the composites was observed with the increase in frequency from 0.1Hz to 10MHz. The dielectric constant of the GQD-PVDF composites was extreme high in low frequency range (>10000 at 0.1Hz). Besides, the dielectric loss was also very high for the GQD-PVDF composites. As to 20%CFO-1%GQD-PVDF ternary composite, the dielectric constant was much higher than that of the CFO-PVDF composites and the dielectric loss was much lower than that of the GQD-PVDF composites. Co-filling of graphene quantum dot and cobalt ferrite nanoparticle creates a three-dimensional structure that guaranteed a composite material with both good flexibility and high dielectric constant, and it could also ensure a reduced dielectric loss. Compared with CFO-PVDF composite, CFO-GQD-PVDF ternary composite showed increased dielectric constant because of more microcapacitors in composite. Compared with GQD-PVDF composite, CFO-GQD-PVDF ternary composite showed decreased dielectric loss because CFO nanoparticles could effectively inhibit the connection of GQD to reduce the leakage current in the graphene network and thus endowed the ternary composite with reduced dielectric loss. The excellent dielectric properties of the CFO-GQD-PVDF ternary composite were beneficial to its applications in micro-electronic field.

In summary, flexible ternary CFO-GQD-PVDF composite materials were fabricated in our present work. The dielectric results showed that the GQD played a key role in the increase of dielectric constant and the presence of CFO particles could guarantee reduced dielectric loss. The ternary structure promised the possibility of fabricating polymer composite material with excellent flexibility, high dielectric constant and low dielectric loss by incorporating two-dimensional conductive fillers, graphene quantum dots and nonconductive fillers, ferrite particles in the polymer matrix. The ternary CFO-GQD-PVDF composite had potential applications as promising candidate materials for embedded capacitors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51603102).
